questions.q: In hypothesis testing, what does the null hypothesis (H₀) typically represent?
options: The claim the researcher hopes to prove
A statement of no effect or no difference
The alternative explanation for the data
The probability the data are random
questions.explanation: The null hypothesis H₀ states that there is no effect, no difference, or no relationship, serving as the default assumed true until evidence contradicts it. The claim the researcher hopes to support is instead the alternative hypothesis.

options: The probability that the null hypothesis is true
The probability of observing data at least as extreme as the sample, assuming H₀ is true
The probability of making a correct decision
The size of the observed effect
questions.explanation: A p-value is the probability of obtaining a result at least as extreme as the observed one, computed under the assumption that the null hypothesis is true. It is not the probability that H₀ itself is true, a common misinterpretation.

questions.q: A Type I error occurs when a researcher does what?
options: Rejects a true null hypothesis
Fails to reject a false null hypothesis
Accepts the alternative when it is true
Uses too small a sample size
questions.explanation: A Type I error is rejecting the null hypothesis when it is actually true (a false positive), and its probability equals the significance level α. Failing to reject a false null hypothesis is instead a Type II error.

questions.q: A Type II error occurs when a researcher does what?
options: Rejects a true null hypothesis
Fails to reject a false null hypothesis
Sets the significance level too high
Reports a confidence interval
questions.explanation: A Type II error is failing to reject the null hypothesis when it is actually false (a false negative), with probability denoted β. Rejecting a true null hypothesis is the opposite mistake, a Type I error.

questions.q: The significance level α of a test corresponds to which quantity?
options: The power of the test
The probability of a Type I error
The probability of a Type II error
The effect size
questions.explanation: The significance level α is the threshold probability of committing a Type I error, the chance of rejecting a true null hypothesis. The probability of a Type II error is β, a separate quantity, and the power of the test equals 1 − β.

questions.q: When would you use a paired t-test rather than a two-sample (independent) t-test?
options: When comparing two unrelated groups
When each observation in one group is naturally matched with one in the other
When the population variance is known
When the sample size exceeds 30
questions.explanation: A paired t-test is used when observations come in matched pairs, such as before-and-after measurements on the same subjects, so it analyzes the differences within pairs. A two-sample t-test instead compares two independent, unrelated groups.

questions.q: The power of a statistical test is defined as which probability?
options: 1 − β, the probability of correctly rejecting a false null
The probability of a Type I error
The p-value at the decision boundary
questions.explanation: Power is 1 − β, the probability that the test correctly rejects the null hypothesis when the alternative is true. It is not 1 − α; that quantity is the confidence level associated with correctly retaining a true null.

questions.q: If a test uses α = 0.05 and the computed p-value is 0.03, what is the conclusion?
options: Fail to reject H₀
Reject H₀
Accept H₀ as proven true
The test is inconclusive
questions.explanation: Because the p-value 0.03 is less than the significance level 0.05, the result is statistically significant and the null hypothesis is rejected. We never accept H₀ as proven; we only reject it or fail to reject it.

questions.q: Which statement best describes a one-tailed test compared to a two-tailed test?
options: It tests for a difference in a specific direction only
It always requires a larger sample size
It tests both directions of an effect simultaneously
It cannot produce a p-value
questions.explanation: A one-tailed test places the entire rejection region in one direction, testing for an effect of a specified sign (for example, greater than). A two-tailed test instead splits the rejection region across both tails to detect a difference in either direction.

questions.q: All else equal, what generally happens to a test's power as the sample size increases?
options: Power decreases
Power increases
Power stays exactly constant
Power equals the significance level
questions.explanation: Larger samples reduce the standard error and make it easier to detect a true effect, so the power (1 − β) increases. Power does not stay constant; it is directly influenced by sample size, effect size, and α.
